text: Backyard chickens require a minimum of 4 square feet of coop space per bird and 10 square feet of outdoor run space. The coop needs roosting bars (8-10 inches per bird, at least 2 feet off the floor), nesting boxes (1 box per 3-4 hens, 12x12x12 inches), adequate ventilation (openings near the roof, not at roost level), and protection from predators. Most laying breeds produce 250-300 eggs per year with 14-16 hours of light daily.

Layer feed should contain 16-18% protein and include calcium supplementation. Provide oyster shell free-choice in a separate container — hens will eat what they need for eggshell production. Fresh water must be available at all times — a hen drinks about 1 pint of water per day, more in hot weather. Supplement with kitchen scraps and garden waste but avoid: avocado, chocolate, raw beans, onions, and citrus in large quantities.

Dairy goats produce 1-3 quarts of milk per day depending on breed. Nigerian Dwarf goats are ideal for small homesteads — compact size, friendly temperament, high butterfat milk (6-10%). Goats must be bred to produce milk and need to be re-bred annually. Typical lactation lasts 10 months. Basic requirements: sturdy fencing (at minimum 4 feet high, goats are escape artists), dry shelter, browse and hay, fresh water, and hoof trimming every 6-8 weeks.

A basic beekeeping setup requires: 2 hive bodies (deep boxes), honey supers, frames with foundation, a bottom board, inner cover, outer cover, smoker, hive tool, and protective gear. Start with 2 hives — if one fails, you can use the other to rebuild. Install package bees or nucleus colonies in early spring when daytime temperatures consistently reach 55°F. Each colony needs approximately 60 pounds of honey to survive winter in cold climates.

Rabbit raising for meat is one of the most efficient small-scale protein sources. A single doe can produce 50-60 pounds of meat per year from 6-8 litters. House rabbits in wire cages (minimum 30x36 inches per adult) elevated off the ground for cleanliness. Feed commercial rabbit pellets (16-18% protein) supplemented with grass hay for fiber. Fresh water via a bottle or automatic waterer. Rabbits are sensitive to heat — provide shade and ventilation when temperatures exceed 85°F.
